content.text: Creating an Azure account provisions an Azure Active Directory (Entra ID) tenant and a first subscription in a single sign-up flow. Every Azure resource you create later lives under one of your subscriptions, which in turn belongs to the tenant. Understanding what gets created — and why — prevents confusion about billing, identity, and permission boundaries from the start.

attrs.alt: Azure account creation flow: 5 steps from signup portal to tenant and subscription

content.text: Signing up for Azure creates two distinct objects:
Has an ID (GUID), linked to the tenant, subject to spending limits and quotas
The Microsoft account / work account used to sign up
Gets Owner role on the subscription by default
One tenant can have many subscriptions. One subscription belongs to exactly one tenant. You can later transfer subscriptions between tenants, but it requires ownership of both.
A valid email address (Microsoft account or work/school account). A new Microsoft account can be created during sign-up.
A phone number for identity verification (SMS or call). VoIP numbers are rejected.
A credit or debit card for identity verification. Free tier accounts will not be charged unless you upgrade; a small authorization hold (~$1 USD) may appear temporarily.
Your country or region must be supported. Go to azure.microsoft.com/en-us/global-infrastructure/geographies to verify.

The Free Account is the most common starting point. It includes a $200 credit valid for 30 days and 12 months of free-tier access to popular services (B1S VM, 5 GB Blob storage, SQL 250 GB DTU, etc.). After the free period, unused free-tier services remain free within limits; the credit expires.
Navigate to the Azure sign-up page and choose Free or Pay-As-You-Go. Click Start free or Start with Pay-As-You-Go.
2. Sign In or Create a Microsoft Account

You need a Microsoft account (personal: @outlook.com, @hotmail.com, or any email enrolled as MSA) or a work/school account (Entra ID or federated). If you do not have one, create it during sign-up. The account you use here becomes the Global Administrator of the new Entra ID tenant and the Owner of the first subscription.

Use a dedicated organizational email rather than a personal email if this account will be used for production workloads. Mixing personal Microsoft accounts with organizational subscriptions complicates governance later.

Azure requests a phone number for SMS or voice verification and a credit/debit card. The card is charged a small temporary hold ($1 or local equivalent) for fraud prevention, which is reversed within a few days. The hold is not a subscription charge.

Review and accept the Microsoft Customer Agreement (MCA). This governs your usage, billing, and data processing. Organizations should review the agreement with legal or procurement before accepting on behalf of the company.

After acceptance, Azure creates your Entra ID tenant (domain: <yourname>.onmicrosoft.com) and your first subscription. You are redirected to the Azure portal (portal.azure.com) where you can immediately begin creating resources.
Verifying the Account via Azure CLI
Once the account is created, install the Azure CLI and verify access:

# Install Azure CLI on Ubuntu/Debian
curl -sL https://aka.ms/InstallAzureCLIDeb | sudo bash

# Install on macOS via Homebrew
brew install azure-cli

# Log in (opens a browser for interactive auth)
az login

# If you have multiple tenants, specify the tenant
az login --tenant <your-tenant-id-or-domain>

# Verify: list subscriptions accessible to this account
az account list --output table

# Show the currently active subscription
az account show

# Show tenant information
az account tenant list -o table

The output of `az account list` should show your new subscription with `IsDefault: True`. The `tenantId` field is your Entra ID tenant GUID — keep it handy for future CLI operations and service principal creation.

For free and PAYG accounts, set a cost budget immediately to prevent surprise charges. Azure does not automatically stop resources when a budget threshold is reached unless you configure action groups.

# Create a budget alert at $10/month with email notification
az consumption budget create \
  --budget-name my-monthly-budget \
  --amount 10 \
  --time-grain Monthly \
  --start-date 2024-01-01 \
  --end-date 2025-01-01 \
  --resource-group '' \
  --notifications '[{"enabled":true,"operator":"GreaterThan","threshold":80,"contactEmails":["you@example.com"],"thresholdType":"Actual"}]'

# Check current spending
az consumption usage list --start-date 2024-01-01 --end-date 2024-01-31 -o table

# Check MFA status for the signed-in account via MS Graph (requires Graph API scope)
az rest --method GET \
  --url 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/me/authentication/methods' \
  --headers 'Content-Type=application/json'

# Enable Entra ID Security Defaults (enforces MFA for all users) via portal:
# Azure Portal -> Entra ID -> Properties -> Manage Security Defaults -> Enable

# Or via MS Graph REST API:
az rest --method PATCH \
  --url 'https://graph.microsoft.com/v1.0/policies/identitySecurityDefaultsEnforcementPolicy' \
  --body '{"isEnabled": true}'
Configure Alert for Root Account Activity

# Create an activity log alert for any sign-in as Owner on the subscription
# First create an action group (email notification)
az monitor action-group create \
  --name ag-security-alerts \
  --resource-group rg-security \
  --short-name secalerts \
  --action email admin you@example.com

# Alert when the subscription Owner role is assigned (sensitive operation)
az monitor activity-log alert create \
  --name alert-owner-role-assign \
  --resource-group rg-security \
  --scope /subscriptions/<sub-id> \
  --condition category=Administrative operationName=Microsoft.Authorization/roleAssignments/write \
  --action-group ag-security-alerts
Understanding the Tenant and Subscription Relationship
New users frequently conflate the Entra ID tenant with the subscription. They are separate objects:
Users, groups, apps, enterprise apps, conditional access

Deleting a subscription does not delete the Entra ID tenant. Deleting the tenant requires first removing all subscriptions, enterprise applications, and users (except the Global Admin doing the deletion).
The Free Account has a spending limit that prevents charges after the $200 credit is exhausted. To move to production workloads, upgrade to Pay-As-You-Go:

# The upgrade is done through the Azure portal: 
# Azure Portal -> Subscriptions -> Select your free subscription -> Upgrade

# After upgrade, verify the offer type changed:
az account show --query '{Name:name, State:state, Id:id}' -o json

# List subscription details including offer
az rest --method GET \
  --url 'https://management.azure.com/subscriptions/<sub-id>?api-version=2022-12-01' \
  --query '{name:displayName, offerType:subscriptionPolicies.quotaId, state:state}'

After upgrading, the spending limit is removed. Services that were free during the trial continue at free-tier rates as long as usage stays within the free-tier quotas. Exceeding free-tier quotas incurs PAYG charges.
Use a mobile carrier number; try a different country prefix
Prepaid card, virtual card, or unsupported issuer
Use a physical credit/debit card from a major bank
Azure not available in your country
Use InPrivate/Incognito mode; clear cookies and retry
Switch directory in portal (top-right menu -> Switch directory)
Previous free trial with same account or card
Use a different Microsoft account and a different card
Azure CLI: First Steps After Account Creation

After signing up, the Azure CLI is the fastest way to explore your new account, inspect what was provisioned, and begin infrastructure work without the portal UI. The following walkthrough covers the first 30 minutes after account creation.

# --- Step 1: Authenticate ---
az login
# For non-interactive (CI/CD) auth using a service principal created later:
# az login --service-principal -u <appId> -p <secret> --tenant <tenantId>

# --- Step 2: Inspect your tenant ---
az account tenant list -o table
# Output columns: TenantId, Name, TenantType (AAD vs B2C vs External)

# --- Step 3: Inspect your subscriptions ---
az account list -o table
# Columns: Name, CloudName, SubscriptionId, State, IsDefault

# --- Step 4: Set the active subscription (if multiple exist) ---
az account set --subscription <subscription-id-or-name>

# --- Step 5: Confirm current context ---
az account show --query '{sub:id, tenant:tenantId, user:user.name}' -o json

# --- Step 6: Create a resource group to test the account ---
az group create --name rg-test --location eastus
az group list -o table

# --- Step 7: Clean up the test resource group ---
az group delete --name rg-test --yes --no-wait

The account owner (Global Administrator + Subscription Owner) should not be used for day-to-day operations. Best practice is to create separate accounts with narrow roles immediately after account creation.

# Create a second administrator user in Entra ID
az ad user create \
  --display-name 'Admin User' \
  --user-principal-name admin@<your-tenant>.onmicrosoft.com \
  --password 'ChangeMe123!' \
  --force-change-password-next-sign-in true

# Get the new user's object ID
USER_OID=$(az ad user show --id admin@<your-tenant>.onmicrosoft.com --query id -o tsv)

# Assign Contributor on the subscription (less than Owner — cannot change RBAC)
az role assignment create \
  --role Contributor \
  --assignee $USER_OID \
  --scope /subscriptions/<sub-id>

# Verify the assignment
az role assignment list \
  --assignee $USER_OID \
  --scope /subscriptions/<sub-id> \
  -o table

# Assign Billing Reader so the user can view costs without resource access
az role assignment create \
  --role 'Billing Reader' \
  --assignee $USER_OID \
  --scope /subscriptions/<sub-id>

Avoid using the sign-up account (which is Global Administrator + Owner) for routine work. Create a named user account with only the roles needed for each task. Use the root account only for tenant-level or subscription-transfer operations.
Subscriptions are the primary billing and resource boundary. Knowing how to inspect and manage subscriptions from day one prevents billing surprises.

# List all subscriptions the signed-in user can access
az account list --all -o table

# Get detailed info on a specific subscription
az account show --subscription <sub-id> -o json

# List subscription-level resource providers and their registration state
az provider list --query '[?registrationState==`Registered`].namespace' -o tsv | head -20

# Register a new resource provider (required before using a new service type)
az provider register --namespace Microsoft.Compute
az provider show --namespace Microsoft.Compute --query registrationState -o tsv

# Check subscription limits (quotas) for a region
az vm list-usage --location eastus -o table

# List available locations for the subscription
az account list-locations -o table

# Get subscription-level resource count
az resource list --query 'length(@)' -o tsv
Establishing resource naming and structural conventions before creating any resources pays off as the account grows. Azure resources have naming constraints and are immutable once named.

A common convention is: `<type>-<workload>-<environment>-<region>[-sequence]`. For example: `rg-api-prod-eastus`, `vm-web-dev-westus-001`. Resource abbreviations are documented in Microsoft's Cloud Adoption Framework naming guide.

# Create a logical initial structure with resource groups
# Separate groups by environment and workload makes RBAC and cost tracking cleaner
for ENV in dev staging prod; do
  az group create \
    --name rg-myapp-${ENV}-eastus \
    --location eastus \
    --tags Environment=${ENV} Application=myapp Owner=platform-team
done

# Verify groups were created
az group list --query '[?starts_with(name, `rg-myapp`)]' -o table
Securing the Account: Recommended Day-One Checklist
Enable Security Defaults in Entra ID (enforces MFA for all users) or configure Conditional Access if you have P1/P2 licensing.
Do not use the sign-up account for daily work — create named user accounts with appropriate roles.
Enable Microsoft Defender for Cloud free tier: provides security score, recommendations, and compliance assessment at no cost.
Configure a budget alert at a safe threshold to avoid unexpected charges.
Review and restrict access to the Entra ID admin portal (admin.microsoft.com) to Global Admins only.
Enable audit logs: Entra ID sign-in logs and Azure Activity logs should be retained in a Log Analytics workspace.
Register only the resource providers you intend to use (reduces attack surface).

# Enable Microsoft Defender for Cloud (free tier) on the subscription
az security auto-provisioning-setting update --name mma --auto-provision on

# Enable Defender for Cloud free tier CSPM (Cloud Security Posture Management)
az security pricing create --name CloudPosture --tier free

# Create a Log Analytics workspace for audit log retention
az monitor log-analytics workspace create \
  --resource-group rg-security-prod-eastus \
  --workspace-name law-audit-prod \
  --location eastus \
  --retention-time 90

# Export Entra ID sign-in logs to the workspace (requires Diagnostic Settings)
az monitor diagnostic-settings create \
  --name ds-aad-logs \
  --resource /tenants/<tenant-id>/providers/microsoft.aadiam \
  --workspace /subscriptions/<sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-security-prod-eastus/providers/Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces/law-audit-prod \
  --logs '[{"category":"SignInLogs","enabled":true},{"category":"AuditLogs","enabled":true}]'

# Check Defender for Cloud secure score
az security secure-score list -o table
Managing the Account with Azure PowerShell
The Az PowerShell module is an alternative to the CLI, preferred in Windows environments and when scripting complex object pipelines. Both modules expose the same Azure Resource Manager APIs.

# Install the Az module (requires PowerShell 7+ or Windows PowerShell 5.1)
Install-Module -Name Az -Scope CurrentUser -Repository PSGallery -Force

# Authenticate interactively
Connect-AzAccount

# List subscriptions
Get-AzSubscription | Format-Table Name, SubscriptionId, State

# Set active subscription
Set-AzContext -SubscriptionId '<sub-id>'

# Get current context
Get-AzContext | Select-Object Name, Subscription, Tenant

# Create a resource group
New-AzResourceGroup -Name 'rg-test-eastus' -Location 'eastus' -Tag @{Environment='test'}

# List all resource groups
Get-AzResourceGroup | Format-Table ResourceGroupName, Location, ProvisioningState

# Check available VM sizes in a region
Get-AzVMSize -Location eastus | Where-Object NumberOfCores -le 4 | Select-Object -First 10

# Remove resource group
Remove-AzResourceGroup -Name 'rg-test-eastus' -Force

After the account is created, Infrastructure as Code (IaC) should be the primary way to create and manage Azure resources. Bicep is Microsoft's domain-specific language for Azure resource deployments, compiling to ARM JSON templates.

# Deploy the Bicep template at subscription scope
az deployment sub create \
  --template-file initial-setup.bicep \
  --location eastus \
  --parameters ownerEmail=you@example.com appName=myapp

# View deployment output
az deployment sub show \
  --name initial-setup \
  --query properties.outputs
Some operations apply to the Entra ID tenant (account-level) and others apply only within a subscription. Misunderstanding this boundary is a common source of confusion for new Azure users.
Add a user to the directory
The Free Account includes 12 months of specific services at no charge, plus always-free services that never expire. Tracking against these limits prevents unexpected charges.
Always-free services (Functions, App Service F1, Cosmos DB free tier, etc.) remain free beyond the 12-month window. Only the time-limited services in the first column revert to paid after 12 months.

# Monitor free-tier usage to stay within limits
# View current month charges by service
az consumption usage list \
  --start-date $(date -d 'first day of month' +%Y-%m-01) \
  --end-date $(date +%Y-%m-%d) \
  --query '[].{Service:instanceName, Cost:pretaxCost, Currency:currency}' \
  -o table

# Get cost breakdown by resource type this month
az consumption usage list \
  --start-date $(date -d 'first day of month' +%Y-%m-01) \
  --end-date $(date +%Y-%m-%d) \
  --query 'sort_by([].{Type:meterDetails.meterName, Cost:pretaxCost}, &Cost) | reverse(@)' \
  -o table | head -20

Creating an Azure account establishes an Entra ID tenant and a first subscription in a single flow. The account owner receives the Owner role on the subscription and Global Administrator role on the tenant. Free Accounts include a $200 credit valid for 30 days and 12 months of free-tier services. After creation, verify access with `az login` and `az account list`, set a budget alert, and enable MFA via Security Defaults. When ready for production, upgrade to Pay-As-You-Go to remove the spending limit.
